abstract: Practical activities remain one of the most effective methodological tools for enhancing the teaching-learning process. Beyond simply sparking an interest in science, these hands-on experiences foster the construction of knowledge and encourage students to develop a critical perspective on the world around them. Despite these benefits, traditional lectures continue to dominate the educational landscape. This study evaluated, characterized, and promoted the use of practical science education within two schools in Vitória de Santo Antão, PE: one rural (Duque de Caxias) and one urban (August 3). Data were collected through site visits and teacher questionnaires. The findings indicate that the Duque de Caxias school possesses better infrastructure and demonstrates a stronger faculty commitment to practical instruction compared to the August 3 school. Both institutions identified a lack of resources, overcrowded classrooms, and limited teacher availability as primary barriers to implementing experimental activities. To address these challenges, specialized laboratory manuals were developed for the 6th through 9th grades to better support both educators and students in the classroom.
